1: "These are the terms of the covenant the LORD commanded Moses to make with the Israelites in Moab, in addition to the covenant he had made with them at Horeb" — the Moab covenant is explicitly presented as a second, additional covenant, not a replacement of Sinai/Horeb. The Hebrew syntax (levad mehabrit asher karat itam bechorev) is emphatic: "besides/in addition to the covenant." This raises a covenantal-theological question that the rabbis debated: is the Moab covenant a renewal of Sinai, an extension, or a distinct covenant? The book of Deuteronomy frames the Moab covenant as the third covenant in sequence: the Abrahamic (Gen 15; 17), the Sinaitic (Exod 19-24), and the Moab covenant — which extends to all future generations (vv14-15). The NT's new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34) is yet another covenant in this progressive sequence.
2: "Moses summoned all Israel and said to them: 'You have seen with your own eyes everything the LORD did in Egypt to Pharaoh, to all his officials, and to all his land'" — the covenant-rehearsal opening: Moses begins the covenant-renewal ceremony with the same rhetorical move as the Decalogue's preamble (Deuteronomy 5:6: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt"). The covenant is grounded in historical acts — what YHWH did, which Israel saw (raitem et kol asher asa). The first-person plural inclusivity of the address extends the Sinai generation's experience to the Moab generation standing before Moses.
3: "Those great trials, the signs and wonders you saw with your own eyes" — the trial-signs-wonders triad for the Exodus miracles (cf. Deuteronomy 4:34; Deuteronomy 7:19; Deuteronomy 26:8): massot gedolot (great trials), ha'otot (the signs), hamoftim (the wonders). The triple description emphasizes both the scale and the evidential nature of the Exodus events. They were designed to be seen (asher ra'u eineicha) — their purpose included the testimony they provided to Israel and the nations about YHWH's character and power. Paul cites the Exodus events as a warning to the church (1 Corinthians 10:1-11: "these things happened to them as examples").
4: "Yet to this day the LORD has not given you a mind that understands or eyes that see or ears that hear" — the incomprehension indictment: despite the miraculous witness of the Exodus, Israel does not comprehend. The inability to understand is attributed to YHWH's not-yet-giving (velo natan YHWH lachem): understanding, seeing eyes, and hearing ears are divine gifts, not natural human faculties. This is not fatalistic determinism but a covenant-theological statement about the depth of human spiritual dullness — even spectacular miracles do not automatically produce understanding. Paul quotes v4 in Romans 11:8 in his discussion of Israel's hardening: "God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear, down to this very day."
5: "For forty years I led you through the wilderness. Your clothes did not wear out, and your sandals did not wear out on your feet" — the wilderness provision as a sign of YHWH's care that should have produced understanding (v4): the miraculous preservation of clothing and sandals for forty years is a quiet miracle compared to the plagues and Red Sea crossing. The ordinariness of the sign is its point — YHWH's care extends to the mundane (clothing, footwear) as well as the spectacular. Nehemiah 9:21 repeats this as part of the covenant-history recitation: a mark of sustained providential care throughout the wilderness generation.
6: "You ate no bread and drank no wine or other fermented drink — so that you would know that I am the LORD your God" — the wilderness diet of manna and water (rather than bread and wine) is a sustained revelatory sign: YHWH sustained Israel with unusual food to demonstrate his identity as provider beyond normal agricultural means. The purpose clause (lema'an teda'u) states the intended pedagogical function of the wilderness experience. The bread-and-wine contrast with manna-and-water will reappear in the NT: Jesus's "bread of life" discourse (John 6) explicitly contrasts himself with the manna, as does the Eucharist's bread-and-wine over against the ordinary food the wilderness generation received.
7: "When you reached this place, Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan came out to fight against us, and we defeated them" — the Transjordanian victories (Numbers 21:21-35; Deuteronomy 2:24-3:11) are cited as the most recent evidence of YHWH's faithfulness before the Jordan crossing. Sihon and Og became the paradigmatic examples of YHWH's military power on Israel's behalf (Psalms 135:11; Deuteronomy 136:19-20; Nehemiah 9:22). Their defeat was the first conquest-territory and demonstrated that the same power that operated in the Exodus was still operative at the boundary of the promised land.
8: "We took their land and gave it as an inheritance to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh" — the Transjordanian tribal allotments (Num 32; Deuteronomy 3:12-20) are mentioned as established facts: the east-Jordan tribes already have their inheritance, demonstrating that YHWH's distribution of the land has begun before the Jordan crossing. The east-Jordan settlement serves as a down-payment and guarantee of the west-Jordan settlement to come. The inclusion of these tribes in the covenant ceremony (vv10-11) ensures that the Transjordanian territory is within the covenant's scope.
9: "Observe the terms of this covenant and carry them out, so that you may be successful in everything you do" — the covenant-obedience imperative: shemartem et divrei habrit hazot (keep the words of this covenant). The verb shamar (keep, guard, observe, H8104) is Deuteronomy's central obedience verb — appearing over 40 times in the book. Joshua 1:7-8 quotes this verse almost exactly in YHWH's commissioning of Joshua: the new leader must keep the Torah book and meditate on it day and night in order to be successful.
10: "All of you are standing today in the presence of the LORD your God — your leaders and chief men, your elders and officials, and all the other men of Israel" — the covenant community catalogue begins: the social hierarchy is enumerated top-to-bottom (rasheichem shivteichem zeqeneichem veshotereichem). The covenant assembly is explicitly structured to include every social stratum. The leaders and officials who might administer the covenant's laws, the elders who provide community wisdom, and the general male population are all named — they all stand before YHWH as covenant-parties, not spectators.
11: "Together with your children and wives, and the foreigners living in your camp who chop your wood and carry your water" — the covenant community's horizontal extension: women and children (who had no legal standing in most ANE covenant and treaty contexts) and the resident foreign laborers at the bottom of the social hierarchy are all explicitly included. The wood-choppers and water-carriers were the lowest-status workers in the camp — possibly Gibeonites (Joshua 9:21-27 describes the Gibeonites as hewers of wood and drawers of water, apparently in fulfillment of this covenant's terms). Their inclusion is a covenant-theological statement: YHWH's covenant reaches even to the margins of the community.
12: "You are here to enter into a covenant with the LORD your God, a covenant the LORD is making with you today and sealing with an oath" — the covenant as an oath-sealed (shevu'ah) agreement: the combination of covenant (brit) and oath (alah) is the suzerainty treaty's binding formula. By swearing an oath, the vassal calls down the curse-consequences upon himself if he violates the stipulations (the curse-list of Deuteronomy 28 is precisely these oath-consequences). YHWH's faithfulness to his covenant promises is assumed; Israel's oath-sworn obligation is what is being renewed here.
13: "To confirm you this day as his people, that he may be your God as he promised you and as he swore to your ancestors Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" — the covenant renewal fulfills the patriarchal promises: the three-patriarch formula connects the Moab covenant to Genesis 17:7 ("I will be your God and the God of your descendants after you"). The covenant formula — "he may be your God" and "you his people" — is the covenant's relational essence in its most compressed form. It appears throughout the OT (Exodus 6:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 7:23; Deuteronomy 31:33; Deuteronomy 32:38; Ezekiel 36:28; Revelation 21:3) as the covenant's permanent goal.
14: "I am making this covenant, with its oath, not only with you who stand here today before the LORD our God" — the temporal extension of the covenant: the Moab covenant is explicitly made not only with those physically present (the generation standing at Moab) but also with those not yet there. This is the covenant's intergenerational extension — the contractual relationship binds descendants who did not personally participate in the ceremony. The same principle is asserted for the Sinai covenant (Deuteronomy 5:2-3: "not with our ancestors... but with us, all of us who are alive here today") and is fundamental to covenant theology's understanding of corporate identity across time.
15: "But also with those who are not here with us today" — the future-generation extension: those not yet born are included in the covenant. Peter's Pentecost sermon quotes this principle (Acts 2:39: "the promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off"). The NT's understanding of the new covenant as available to "all nations" (Matthew 28:19) and to "Jew and Greek" (Romans 1:16) is the universal extension of this intergenerational-extension principle. The covenant's temporal reach across generations is matched in the new covenant by a spatial reach across nations.
16: "You remember how we lived in Egypt and how we traveled through the nations on your way here" — the anti-idolatry argument from experience: Israel has been through Egypt and through the Transjordanian nations, and has seen their religious practices firsthand. The memory of pagan nations' worship systems is not to stimulate nostalgia but to inoculate against temptation. The reminder that Israel saw these things with their own eyes continues the witness-language of the chapter: covenant faithfulness is grounded in what Israel has actually seen of both YHWH's acts (vv2-3) and pagan alternatives (vv16-18).
17: "You saw their detestable images — their idols of wood, stone, silver, and gold" — the idol-materials catalogue: wood and stone (the natural, low-cost materials) alongside silver and gold (the high-value cult-images). The four-material list encompasses the full range of idol-manufacture. Deuteronomy's consistent characterization of idols as human-made objects of wood, stone, and metal (Deuteronomy 4:28; Deuteronomy 28:36,64; Deuteronomy 29:17) strips them of divine pretense: they are craft-objects, not deities. Isaiah 40-44 develops this critique most fully: the same tree provides material for a fire and for a god (Isaiah 44:16-17) — an absurdity.
18: "Make sure there is no man or woman, clan or tribe among you today whose heart turns away from the LORD our God to go and worship the gods of those nations; make sure there is no root among you that produces such bitter poison" — the "root of bitterness" (shoresh poreh rosh veleanah) — a root that bears gall and bitterness. The phrase is quoted in Hebrews 12:15: "See to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God; that no 'root of bitterness' springs up and causes trouble, and by it many become defiled." The root-metaphor is apt: apostasy begins underground, hidden in the heart, before it surfaces in visible worship.
19: "One who hears the terms of this oath and yet reassures himself, thinking, 'I will be safe, even though I persist in going my own way'" — the self-reassuring covenant-member: the internal rationalization that covenant membership provides safety regardless of personal faithfulness. The phrase "I will be safe" (shalom yihyeh li, lit. "peace will be to me") is the false prophet's message in Jeremiah 6:14 and Deuteronomy 8:11: "Peace, peace" when there is no peace. The person in v19 applies the false-prophet's logic to himself — claiming covenant-blessing language (shalom) for his own disobedient condition. The NT parallel: those who claim covenant identity without covenant transformation (Romans 2:17-24; James 2:14-17).
20: "The LORD will never pardon him. Instead, the LORD's anger and his jealousy will burn against that person; all the curses written in this book will fall on him, and the LORD will blot out his name from under heaven" — the unpardonable self-rationalizing apostate: the one who has heard the covenant and deliberately chosen to transgress while claiming covenant safety will not be pardoned. The phrase "blot out his name" (macha et shemo) is the formal removal from the Book of Life — the covenant-register (cf. Exodus 32:32-33; Psalms 69:28; Revelation 3:5). The divine jealousy (qin'ato, H7068) is the exclusive-covenant claim YHWH makes on his people — the same jealousy that grounds the second commandment's prohibition of idolatry (Deuteronomy 5:9).
21: "The LORD will single him out from all the tribes of Israel for disaster, in keeping with all the curses of the covenant written in this Book of the Law" — the individual singled out from the collective: the apostasy of an individual within the covenant community is not covered by the community's covenant. The singling-out (hivdil YHWH oto lera'ah) for evil from among the tribes is the negative application of Israel's being set apart (hivdil) from the nations for holiness (Leviticus 20:24,26). The same divine act that separated Israel as holy from the nations now separates the apostate as condemned from Israel.
22: "Your descendants who follow you and foreigners who come from distant lands will see the calamities that fall on that land and the diseases the LORD has inflicted on it" — the international witness to judgment: the nations observe Israel's punishment and recognize its divine cause. The future-generations perspective sets the curse's impact in a broader temporal frame: the land's devastation will be visible and interpretable for centuries. This picks up the theme from Deuteronomy 28:37 (Israel becomes a horror and byword among the nations) but now frames the nations' observation as leading to a theological explanation (vv24-25).
23: "They will see the whole land laid waste with burning sulfur and salt — nothing planted, nothing sprouting, no vegetation growing on it. It will be like the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, which the LORD overthrew in fierce anger" — the Sodom-comparison is the ultimate ancient benchmark for divine judgment and total destruction. The four cities of the plain (Genesis 14:2) were destroyed by fire and sulfur (Genesis 19:24). Salt and sulfur soil is permanently sterile. The comparison is applied in Isaiah 1:9, Deuteronomy 13:19; Jeremiah 49:18; Deuteronomy 50:40; Amos 4:11; Zephaniah 2:9. The Sodom-standard became a set phrase for the most complete imaginable divine destruction.
24: "All the nations will ask: 'Why has the LORD done this to this land? Why this fierce, burning anger?'" — the nations' question frames vv22-28 as an international theodicy: observers of Israel's devastation will ask the theological question "why?" The question implies that the nations recognize YHWH as the agent of the destruction — not Babylon or Assyria as the ultimate cause but YHWH acting through them. The same question-and-answer structure appears in 1 Kings 9:8-9, Jeremiah 22:8-9, and 2 Chronicles 7:21-22.
25: "And the answer will be: 'It is because this people abandoned the covenant of the LORD, the God of their ancestors, the covenant he made with them when he brought them out of Egypt'" — the official theological interpretation of the exile: covenant-abandonment. The answer is given not by Israelites but by the nations — even foreigners can read the theological meaning of Israel's historical catastrophe from the documentary evidence of the covenant-curse structure. The Deuteronomistic History (Judges through 2 Kings) presents itself as the fulfillment of exactly this prophesied explanation: the fall of Israel (722 BCE) and Judah (586 BCE) are both interpreted as covenant-abandonment (2 Kings 17:7-23; Deuteronomy 24:2-4).
26: "They went and worshiped other gods and bowed down to them — gods they had not known, gods he had not given them" — the precise breach: worshiping gods YHWH had not authorized (asher lo yadaum, which they had not known; asher lo chalaq lahem, which he had not allotted to them). The "allotted to them" phrase may refer to Deuteronomy 4:19's statement that YHWH allotted the celestial bodies to other nations — Israel's allotment was YHWH himself, not the stellar deities. To worship the allotted-for-others gods was to transgress the covenant's territorial principle with catastrophic consequences.
27: "Therefore the LORD's anger burned against this land, so that he brought on it all the curses written in this book" — the direct causation stated: the curses of Deuteronomy 28 are not arbitrary historical misfortune but covenant-documented, prophesied consequences of covenant-breach. The phrase "all the curses written in this book" makes the theological argument: what the nations observe (vv22-23) and what the nations explain (vv24-26) is the fulfillment of documented covenant-stipulations. History is covenant-theology made visible in time.
28: "In furious anger and great wrath the LORD uprooted them from their land and thrust them into another land, as it is now" — the exile as a completed act: the text anticipates the final state. The phrase "as it is now" (kayom hazeh) is either a late editorial note reflecting the Babylonian exile's completion, or a prophetic present-tense anticipation of the exile's certainty. Jeremiah 24:6 uses the same uprooting/transplanting metaphor ("I will plant them and not uproot them") for the restoration — the exile's uprooting is not permanent but is itself a covenant action that has a restoration-counterpart (Deuteronomy 30:1-10).
29: "The secret things belong to the LORD our God, but the things revealed belong to us and to our children forever, so that we may follow all the words of this law" — the hermeneutical capstone of the Moab covenant: two categories of divine reality are distinguished. The hidden things (hanistarot) — YHWH's undisclosed purposes, his eternal decrees, the timing of future events — belong to YHWH alone. The revealed things (haniglot) — the Torah, the covenant stipulations, the blessing-and-curse structure — belong to Israel permanently. The verse sets the boundaries of both human knowledge and human responsibility: not everything is knowable, but everything necessary for obedience is known. This is one of the most important epistemological principles in the OT: covenant responsibility is bounded by covenant revelation. Paul's doxology in Romans 11:33-36 ("Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments and how inscrutable his ways!") is the NT equivalent — some things are beyond human comprehension; what is revealed is sufficient for faith and practice.
